title: More Regex Patterns
description: Character classes, quantifiers, anchors, and groups.

slides.title: Regex Building Blocks
slides.content:
# Common Pattern Elements

*   **Character classes**: `[abc]` matches a, b, or c. `[^abc]` matches anything except a,b,c.
*   **Ranges**: `[a-z]` lowercase letters, `[0-9]` digits.
*   **Predefined classes**: `\d` digit, `\w` word character (letter, digit, underscore), `\s` whitespace.
*   **Quantifiers**: `*` zero or more, `+` one or more, `?` zero or one, `{n}` exactly n, `{n,}` at least n, `{n,m}` between n and m.
*   **Anchors**: `^` start of string, `$` end of string.
*   **Groups**: `(pattern)` capturing group, `(?:pattern)` non-capturing group.

slides.title: Examples of Patterns
preg_match_all()
slides.content:
# Finding All Matches

`preg_match_all()` returns the number of full matches, and fills `$matches` with all occurrences.

```php
<?php
$text = "The numbers: 123, 456, 789";
preg_match_all("/\d+/", $text, $matches);
print_r($matches[0]); // ["123", "456", "789"]
?>
```

slides.title: preg_match_all Example
slides.content:
```php First

Second

Third

"; preg_match_all("/

(.*?)<\/p>/", $html, $matches); print_r($matches[1]); // ["First", "Second", "Third"] ?> ``` Note the lazy quantifier `*?` to stop at the first closing tag.

slides.title: Regex Performance
slides.content:
## Beware of Catastrophic Backtracking

Some regex patterns can be extremely slow on certain inputs (e.g., `/(a+)+$/`). Keep patterns simple, and test with edge cases.

quizData.question: Which pattern matches a string consisting of exactly three digits?
quizData.explanation: `/^\d{3}$/` matches start, three digits, end – so exactly three digits.
